content.problemStatement:
Oh, these difficult contests... In each contest, there are so many hard tasks Chef cannot solve. However, he knows that he can only become a successful coder if he works hard. Fortunately, he has a problem in mind for training.

You are given two positive integers aaa and bbb. You may apply the following magical operation any number of times (including zero):


choose an arbitrary **positive** integer ddd
perform exactly one of the following actions:

add ddd to aaa, add d−1d-1d−1 to bbb
add d−1d-1d−1 to aaa, add ddd to bbb


Chef is asking you to find out if it is possible to obtain a=ba = ba=b. Help him!
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers a and b.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the string "YES" if it is possible to obtain a=b or "NO" if it is impossible.

sampleTestCases.explanation: We can make a and b equal by adding 1 to a and 2 to b, then adding 2 to a and 1 to b, and so on.
The difference between a and b is 2, which is even. However, we cannot make a and b equal because the difference is not a multiple of 2.
a and b are already equal, so we do not need to do anything.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we need to check if the difference between a and b is even. If it is, then we can make a and b equal by adding the same number to both a and b. If the difference is odd, then it is impossible to make a and b equal.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) — we are looping over T test cases, and for each test case, we are performing a constant amount of work.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — we are using a constant amount of space to store the input and output.
